title: Bulk and surface excitons in the van der Waals magnet CrSBr: Magneto-optical studies to 55 tesla
authors: Junho Choi, Yihyun Moon, Doohyeon Lee, Iva Plutnarova, Zdenek Sofer, Vinod M. Menon, Scott A. Crooker
abstract: In thin layers of the 2D magnetic semiconductor CrSBr, very recent studies identified two distinct band-edge optical resonances, believed to arise from distinguishable bulk and surface excitons. This behavior reportedly originates from the highly anisotropic nature of CrSBr -- particularly in its antiferromagnetic state -- where excitons are effectively confined within individual monolayers, such that excitons in the two surface layers "see" a different local dielectric environment and have a lower resonance energy. To explore this scenario, here we investigate optical absorption properties of few-layer CrSBr in magnetic fields. In addition to the fundamental exciton resonance at ~1.36eV, we observe an absorption resonance ~20 meV lower in energy. Compared to the fundamental transition, this resonance redshifts only half as much in small magnetic fields that induce ferromagnetic order, while in high fields to 55T it exhibits a smaller diamagnetic shift. Both behaviors point to distinguishable populations of bulk and surface excitons in CrSBr.
summary: This research uses magneto-optical spectroscopy on few-layer CrSBr to distinguish between bulk and surface excitons. By measuring how two distinct exciton resonances shift under applied magnetic fields, the authors provide strong evidence that surface excitons are physically and electronically different from their bulk counterparts. This work advances the understanding of exciton physics in low-dimensional magnetic semiconductors.
main_problem: To investigate and validate the existence and properties of distinguishable bulk and surface excitons in the van der Waals magnet CrSBr using magneto-optical measurements.
main_result: The study confirms two distinct exciton resonances—a fundamental bulk exciton and a lower-energy surface exciton—whose differing responses to magnetic fields (redshift and diamagnetic shift) provide strong evidence for their distinct physical origins.
method: Polarized optical absorption spectroscopy was performed on few-layer CrSBr samples under varying magnetic fields, up to 55 Tesla.
model_or_system: The system is few-layer CrSBr, a 2D magnetic semiconductor. The model posits that weak inter-layer coupling confines excitons, leading to surface excitons experiencing a different local dielectric environment than the bulk-like excitons.
key_observables: Exciton resonance energies ($	ext{X}_{	ext{bulk}}$ vs. $	ext{X}_{	ext{surf}}$), redshift magnitude in low fields, and diamagnetic shift ($\Delta E_{	ext{dia}}$) in high fields.
important_parameters: Magnetic field strength (up to 55 T), layer thickness (2L to 18L), and the difference in local dielectric environment between surface and bulk layers.
main_assumptions: The spectral differences are attributed to the distinct local dielectric environments experienced by surface versus bulk excitons.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate optical absorption spectra showing two resonances ($	ext{X}_{	ext{bulk}}$ and $	ext{X}_{	ext{surf}}$) and demonstrate how their respective energies shift differently under applied magnetic fields (low-field redshift and high-field diamagnetic shift) as a function of layer number.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes optical absorption spectra across varying layer thicknesses and magnetic fields. It first establishes the existence of two resonances, then uses low-field magneto-optics to compare redshift magnitudes, and finally uses high-field measurements to compare diamagnetic shifts, confirming the surface/bulk distinction.
why_interesting: The work provides a clear experimental demonstration of how dimensionality and local environment (dielectric screening) can lead to distinct, measurable quantum excitations (excitons) in 2D magnetic materials, which is highly relevant for designing novel quantum optical devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a) Illustration of bulk and surface excitons (Xbulk, Xsurf) in a hBN-encapsulated N-layer CrSBr sample. Exci- tons are largely confined within individual monolayers, due to weak interlayer coupling. Excitons in surface layers see a different local dielectric environment due to the neighboring hBN (ϵhBN < ϵCrSBr), leading to weaker screening and slightly different resonance energy. Blue, yellow, red spheres denote Cr, S, Br atoms. b) Schematic of the sample-on-fiber assem- bly, which enables absorbance spectroscopy of exfoliated vdW materials in high (pulsed) magnetic fields (see main text). c) Photograph of an encapsulated 4L CrSBr sample, affixed over the fiber’s 5 µm diameter core...

Figure 1d shows the optical absorption spectrum, cal- culated as −ln(T/T0), of a 4-layer CrSBr sample at low temperature (4 K) and zero field (B=0). To best cap- ture the exciton absorption, the transmission spectrum T and the reference spectrum T0 were acquired using light linearly polarized along the crystal’s ˆb and ˆa axis (where band-edge exciton absorption in CrSBr is max-

FIG. 2. a) Low-temperature absorption spectra from 4L CrSBr with increasing B (∥ˆc) from 0 →2 T (in steps of ∼0.2 T). Both Xbulk and Xsurf resonances redshift to lower energies as the CrSBr reorients from antiferromagnetic to fer- romagnetic order. The redshift of Xsurf (∼6 meV) is slightly less than half that of Xbulk (∼14 meV). The red ovals in the cartoon insets represent their spatial envelope wavefunctions ψsurf(r) and ψbulk(r) within the CrSBr sample, and depict how ψbulk(r) delocalizes in both +ˆc and −ˆc directions when interlayer coupling becomes more likely in the ferromagnetic state, whereas ψsurf(r) can delocalize in only the +ˆc direction (or −ˆc for the top surface), leading...

FIG. 3. a) Absorption of the 4L CrSBr sample in high mag- netic fields to ±55 T, using circularly polarized light. b,c) The quadratic diamagnetic shift of the bulk and surface ex- citon resonances. The diamagnetic shift of Xsurf is 25% less than that exhibited by Xbulk, consistent with the reduced dielectric screening of Xsurf due to the hBN encapsulation. d) Calculated radial probability density of bulk and surface exciton wavefunctions in hBN-encapsulated CrSBr (see main text). e) Calculated ratio σsurf/σbulk for CrSBr, versus di- electric constant of the surrounding environment, for different exciton reduced masses µ.

FIG. 4. a) The four panels show low-temperature optical absorption spectra from a 2L, 3L, 4L, and a thick (∼18L) CrSBr sample respectively, as B ∥ˆc increases from 0 →2 T (in steps of ≈0.2 T). Note that the 2L sample lacks the central absorption resonance from Xbulk, because no “bulk-like” layers exist in 2L CrSBr. b) These panels show the corresponding B-dependent energies of the absorption resonances. The net redshifts of the resonances (between 0 T and ∼2 T) are indicated. In all cases, the redshift of Xsurf is slightly less than half the redshift of Xbulk. c) These panels show the high-field diamagnetic shifts of these absorption resonances, along with best quadratic fits.
